explanation: The formula requires that prop_0 can happen only after prop_1 has occurred, and before prop_1 occurs, the proposition prop_2 must hold. This is captured by stating that before (and up to) the first occurrence of prop_1, prop_2 holds globally, and prop_0 cannot occur before prop_1. Once prop_1 has happened, prop_0 is allowed.

The requirement means that 'prop_0' can only happen after 'prop_1' has occurred. Moreover, up until 'prop_1' occurs, 'prop_2' should hold. This can be captured by enforcing: (1) 'prop_0' never occurs before 'prop_1', and (2) before 'prop_1', 'prop_2' must be true. In LTL, 'prop_0' is forbidden until 'prop_1' occurs (i.e., (!prop_1) U prop_0 is false), while 'prop_2' holds until 'prop_1' occurs.

prop_0 can only occur after prop_1, and until prop_1 happens, prop_2 must hold. In LTL, this can be captured by forcing that prop_0 is not true until after prop_1, and before prop_1 happens prop_2 holds globally.

This statement says that prop_0 cannot happen before prop_1, and before prop_1, the system satisfies prop_2. In LTL: 'Until prop_1, prop_0 cannot happen and prop_2 must hold', which is: (!prop_1 U (prop_2 & !prop_0)) ensures 'no prop_0 or prop_2 must hold until prop_1'. Alternatively, globally, if not yet prop_1, then not prop_0 and prop_2 hold. So, G( (!prop_1) -> (!prop_0 & prop_2) ) enforces at all times before prop_1, prop_0 does not happen and prop_2 does.

The statement requires that prop_0 cannot occur until after prop_1 has occurred, and before prop_1 has occurred, prop_2 must hold. This is equivalent to saying: Until prop_1 happens, prop_2 must be true, and prop_0 cannot be true before prop_1.

This requirement means that prop_0 may only occur after prop_1 has happened (that is, prop_1 must be true at some point before or at the same time as the first prop_0). Until prop_1 occurs, prop_2 must be true. That is, prop_2 must hold up to and including the point where prop_1 becomes true, and prop_0 must not occur before prop_1.

The formula expresses that prop_0 can only occur after prop_1 has already occurred, and before prop_1, only prop_2 may occur. That is, until prop_1, prop_0 does not happen; after prop_1, prop_0 is allowed. So, globally, either prop_1 has not yet occurred (and if so, prop_0 does not occur, and only prop_2 may occur), or after prop_1, prop_0 is allowed. In LTL: G ( ( (!prop_1) -> (!prop_0 & prop_2) ) )

"prop_0" is allowed to occur only after "prop_1" has occurred, and until "prop_1" has occurred, "prop_2" must hold. This can be encoded by saying that until (prop_1), prop_0 doesn't happen and prop_2 always holds; after prop_1, prop_0 may happen any time. This matches: (G((!prop_1) -> (!prop_0 & prop_2))).

The statement says 'prop_0' can only happen after 'prop_1', and until 'prop_1' happens, 'prop_2' holds. This is represented by: (1) Whenever 'prop_0' is true, in the past 'prop_1' must have occurred (so, G (prop_0 -> F prop_1) but we can use '!prop_0 U prop_1' to enforce that) and (2) 'prop_2' holds up to (but not including) 'prop_1' (so, '!prop_1 -> (prop_2 U prop_1)') or globally: 'G ((!prop_1) -> (prop_2 U prop_1))'. So, putting this together: 'prop_0' does not occur before 'prop_1', and before 'prop_1', 'prop_2' holds.

explanation: We want to specify that 'prop_0' can only occur after 'prop_1' has occurred, and until 'prop_1' occurs, 'prop_2' holds. This means: (1) Once 'prop_1' happens, before that, 'prop_0' is not allowed; (2) Before 'prop_1', 'prop_2' holds globally. So, always before 'prop_1', 'prop_2' holds (G (!prop_1 -> prop_2)), and 'prop_0' cannot become true before 'prop_1' (G (!prop_1 -> !prop_0)). Once 'prop_1' has happened, nothing in particular is specified.
